title: Implement lazygit-style Unicode commit graph renderer
description:
Implement lazygit-style Unicode commit graph rendering in repository commit history.

Why this exists:
The current Rust implementation only stores raw `git log --graph` output and shows a tiny 3-row window around the selected commit. That proves graph mode exists, but it does not match lazygit's commit graph quality or readability. Upstream lazygit uses a dedicated renderer that builds per-cell graph geometry and outputs box-drawing Unicode such as `│`, `─`, `╭`, `╮`, `╰`, `╯`, `┬`, `┴`, plus `◯` for commits and `⏣` for merges. The current app therefore falls short of the intended lazygit-grade UX.

Evidence gathered from references:
- `references/lazygit-master/pkg/gui/presentation/graph/cell.go` maps graph connections to box-drawing Unicode glyphs.
- `references/lazygit-master/pkg/gui/presentation/graph/graph.go` renders graph lines through `RenderAux(...)`, meaning lazygit is not just dumping `git log --graph`.
- `references/lazygit-master/pkg/gui/presentation/graph/graph_test.go` contains expected output strings using `│`, `╭`, `╰`, `┬`, `┴`, `◯`, and `⏣`.
- Local code currently builds graph strings from raw CLI output in `crates/git/src/lib.rs` and only displays a narrow slice in `crates/tui/src/lib.rs`.

What to build:
- Add a proper commit graph renderer on the Rust side instead of relying on raw `git log --graph` text.
- Model enough graph structure to render branch continuations, forks, merges, and selected-commit emphasis cleanly.
- Use Unicode box-drawing glyphs comparable to lazygit's terminal rendering, not plain ASCII fallback as the default path.
- Expand the TUI commit history presentation so the graph is visible as a meaningful pane/list column rather than a 3-line preview fragment.
- Preserve commit metadata readability: hash/summary/refs should remain aligned and usable after the graph is upgraded.
- Keep graph rendering deterministic and testable from pure Rust unit tests.

Implementation guidance:
- Start by studying upstream `graph.go` and `cell.go` in `references/lazygit-master/pkg/gui/presentation/graph/` and port the rendering model rather than inventing an unrelated graph shape.
- Decide whether the graph engine belongs in `crates/git` (data extraction + graph topology) or `crates/tui` (presentation-only). The likely clean split is: commit topology in `crates/git`, glyph rendering/presentation adaptation in a shared helper or TUI-facing layer.
- Avoid coupling graph output to terminal color escape sequences in backend data structures. Prefer structured graph state and let TUI apply styling.
- Ensure all-branches graph mode still works and does not regress linear history mode.
- Consider whether selected commit highlighting should mirror lazygit's "selected parent path" emphasis in a later subtask; if not included now, explicitly document the gap.

Acceptance criteria:
- Commit history graph no longer depends on raw `git log --graph` text formatting as its primary rendered output.
- Rendered graph uses Unicode line/corner/junction glyphs in the default terminal path.
- Merge commits and branching paths are visually distinguishable and remain stable across scrolling/selection changes.
- The commit history UI exposes enough rows/width that the upgraded graph is actually useful to a human.
- Existing non-graph commit history behavior keeps working.

Testing expectations:
- Unit tests covering graph cell/glyph generation for linear history, branch fork, merge, multi-branch continuation, and left/right movement cases.
- Snapshot-like tests for representative rendered lines, similar in spirit to upstream lazygit graph tests.
- TUI rendering test that verifies the repository commit view includes the richer graph output instead of only a tiny preview window.
- If performance becomes an issue, add a benchmark or at least a regression test for moderate commit counts.

Notes:
- This is a parity/UX feature, but it is also a correctness/readability issue because current graph mode suggests a capability that is much weaker than users expect from lazygit.
- If there is any terminal/font fallback path, keep it additive; do not downgrade the main path away from Unicode rendering.

title: Add previous-branch checkout shortcut parity in branch detail
close remaining lazygit clone-1:1 repo-global and files-surface gaps
description:
Close the remaining lazygit clone-1:1 gaps that sit outside the already-landed first-pass repo-interior work. The earlier parity pass created the main panels and landed several shortcut families, but a stricter audit against upstream keybindings and workflows shows that the project still lacks a canonical backlog for exact repo-global behavior, shared list navigation, files/main-panel semantics, and commit-files/custom-patch behavior.

Why this epic exists now:
- the product goal has shifted from "very close" parity to a deliberate 1:1 lazygit clone for repo-mode UI/UX whenever Git correctness and terminal constraints permit it
- finishing the current `supergit-3o1.*` set still would not make the shipped UX feel exactly like lazygit because several global and files-surface behaviors remain either absent or only loosely approximated
- the backlog must become exhaustive enough that we do not need another manual audit just to discover missing UI/UX work

This epic should own every remaining repo-global, shared-navigation, files/main-panel, and commit-files/custom-patch gap that is still visible when comparing the shipped app against upstream lazygit.
acceptance_criteria: Done when every remaining lazygit-facing gap outside the repo-interior panel families is represented by an open child bead with explicit scope, testing obligations, and dependencies; no reviewer should need a fresh audit to discover missing UI/UX work in these surfaces; any unavoidable non-parity must be captured by an explicit blocker or follow-up bead instead of staying implicit.

title: add repo-global command, navigation, and option-menu clone parity
description:
Add the remaining repo-global lazygit command layer with clone-1:1 intent instead of broad "near parity" intent. This bead should be the umbrella for exact keybinding, modal, and toggle parity that is still missing after the first pass.

The remaining global surface includes recent-repo switching, command log and shell command entry, filter and diff option menus, merge/rebase option entry points, whitespace and diff-context toggles, rename-similarity controls, pager and screen-mode cycling, suspend support where admitted, and any other top-level controls lazygit users expect before they even descend into panel-specific workflows.

This bead should also own the requirement that all global commands are discoverable in help/docs and regression-tested as user-facing behavior rather than treated as internal implementation details.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the remaining repo-global lazygit command surface is represented by exact child beads, keybinding/help copy is truthful, and the project has an explicit path to either ship or consciously track every top-level lazygit command that still differs from upstream.

title: clone lazygit global command, option-menu, and toggle surfaces exactly
description:
Clone lazygit's remaining global command, option-menu, and toggle surfaces exactly. This task exists because the current backlog names the command families but still leaves too much room for an implementation that is merely adjacent to lazygit instead of matching it.

Required clone scope:
- recent-repo switching via `<c-r>` including honest integration with the workspace/repo model
- command log options `@` and shell-command entry `:`
- merge/rebase options entry `m` and custom-patch options entry `<c-p>` where they map to real shipped flows
- diff/filter option menus `<c-s>`, `W`, `<c-e>`
- whitespace toggle `<c-w>`, rename-similarity controls `(` and `)`, diff-context controls `{` and `}`
- pager and screen-mode cycling (`|`, `+`, `_`) plus refresh/suspend semantics (`R`, `<c-z>`) where supported in terminal constraints

The task must treat exact keybinding parity, help visibility, and safety semantics as product scope, not documentation cleanup.
design: Implement this as a shared global command layer rather than one-off shortcuts. Reuse modal/menu primitives where possible, but make the resulting user experience line up with upstream lazygit terminology, discoverability, and defaults. If a control truly cannot be cloned yet, create an explicit blocker or follow-up bead instead of silently narrowing scope.
acceptance_criteria: Done when every remaining lazygit global command/toggle/menu listed above is either implemented with exact key semantics or split into an explicit blocker bead; reducer/router/help tests cover them; and the operator can discover and use them without relying on memory of upstream lazygit.
notes: Add detailed reducer, routing, render/help, and end-to-end coverage with logs for command invocation, cancellation, and failure reporting. Failure modes to treat explicitly: unsupported terminals, unsafe shell execution boundaries, missing recent-repo data, and stateful toggles drifting out of sync with the rendered UI.
close_reason: Completed feasible global parity slice; remaining unsupported parity surfaces split into follow-up beads

title: clone lazygit shared list navigation, paging, and range-selection semantics
description:
Clone lazygit's shared list navigation, paging, range-selection, and tab-traversal semantics across the repo-mode surfaces that still behave more narrowly than upstream. The missing behavior is not panel-specific; it is a repeated interaction contract that affects discoverability and muscle memory everywhere.

Required clone scope:
- previous/next page via `,` and `.` where list density makes paging meaningful
- scroll-to-top / scroll-to-bottom via `<` and `>` / home/end semantics
- range select via `v` plus shifted movement when the surface supports multi-selection
- horizontal scroll semantics `H` / `L` where panels overflow
- next/previous tab semantics `]` / `[` where lazygit treats neighboring views as tabs
- exact search/focus-return behavior for list surfaces that should inherit the shared contract
design: Treat this as a cross-surface navigation contract. Do not bury paging or range selection inside one panel. The result should make files, commit lists, commit-files, stash-files, and other list-heavy surfaces behave consistently enough that a lazygit user can transfer muscle memory without thinking.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the shared list-navigation model is implemented or explicitly ruled out per-surface with follow-up beads, and no repeated lazygit navigation primitive remains untracked.
notes: Add reducer/TUI route tests for every shared navigation primitive and at least one end-to-end script that exercises paging, range selection, horizontal scroll, and tab traversal across multiple panel families with detailed logging.

title: implement lazygit merge/rebase and custom patch option menus parity
close_reason: Completed merge/rebase and patch option menus by wiring m/ctrl+p to shipped flows

add commit-files and custom-patch clone parity tracking
description:
Bring commit-files and custom-patch behavior up to a clone-1:1 lazygit target. The earlier commit-history bead established commit browsing and a basic changed-files explorer, but lazygit's commit-files surface is much richer: tree/flat layout, include toggles for custom patches, file and directory drill-down semantics, clipboard/open/difftool affordances, and a line-granular patch builder that must feel native rather than bolted on.

This bead should own the exactness requirement for commit-files UX rather than allowing the project to stop at a thinner explorer that only resembles lazygit from a distance.
acceptance_criteria: Done when commit-files and custom-patch behavior are broken down into exact clone-parity child beads, and the remaining work is explicit enough that nobody can mistake the current explorer for finished lazygit-equivalent behavior.

title: clone lazygit commit-files explorer and custom-patch builder exactly
description:
Clone lazygit's commit-files explorer and custom-patch builder exactly instead of treating it as a thin changed-files list. Upstream lazygit uses this surface for archaeology, selective patch construction, directory-tree traversal, and file-level actions that are central to advanced history editing.

Required clone scope:
- exact tree/flat controls, collapse/expand semantics, and directory-entry behavior
- toggle-inclusion semantics for files and all-files (`Space`, `a`)
- `Enter` behavior for entering files and descending into finer-grained patch editing
- clipboard, checkout, discard-from-commit, open, edit, and external-difftool affordances
- the line-granular custom-patch builder behavior reached from commit-files, including search and exit semantics
design: Do not stop at tree rendering. The real goal is the full custom-patch workflow and its surrounding ergonomics. Reuse shared explorers only if they can preserve the exact lazygit mental model for inclusion state, directory collapse, and line-level patch editing.
acceptance_criteria: Done when commit-files and custom-patch behavior are clone-level enough that a lazygit user can build and edit patches without discovering hidden omissions mid-flow.
notes: Add reducer/backend/e2e coverage for file inclusion toggles, directory collapse, entry into line-level patch mode, discard safety, and patch-builder exit/re-entry. Logs should make it easy to diagnose mismatches in inclusion state and navigation.

title: finish files and main-panel clone parity
description:
Finish the main files surface and its paired main-panel behaviors to a clone-1:1 lazygit target. The current app already supports stage/unstage, commit flows, stash variants, and several detail-pane shortcuts, but lazygit still exposes a denser files-panel interaction model: exact file-tree behavior, status filters, stage-all flows, ignore/exclude, main-panel search and focus return, fixup-base discovery, merge/conflict menus, and precise movement between files and main panels.

This bead should be the umbrella for every files-surface behavior users hit before or while entering staging/merge/search flows.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the files surface and its main-panel interactions are represented by exact child beads and no meaningful lazygit files/main-panel UX gap remains untracked.

title: clone lazygit files panel exact action and tree semantics
description:
Clone lazygit's files panel exact action and tree semantics. The current app exposes the main files surface, but upstream lazygit still has a denser interaction model that drives daily muscle memory.

Required clone scope:
- exact status filtering via `<c-b>`
- stage-all / unstage-all via `a`
- ignore or exclude flow via `i`
- file-tree toggle and collapse/expand controls via `` ` ``, `-`, and `=`
- exact `Enter` behavior for file-vs-directory selection, including collapse/expand and transition into main-panel staging flows
- remaining files-panel actions lazygit exposes: copy path/clipboard, open in default app, external editor, external difftool, refresh, find-base-commit-for-fixup, upstream reset options, merge/conflict options, `0`, `/`, and truthful help copy
design: Implement this as exact files-panel behavior, not a loose approximation. Where the current 3-pane layout differs from lazygit's visual split, preserve the lazygit semantics anyway and document the mapping clearly in help text and the parity matrix.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the files panel no longer has any meaningful lazygit-visible action or tree-behavior gap left untracked, and the exact key semantics are backed by route/reducer/help coverage.
notes: Use integration fixtures with mixed staged/unstaged/untracked/renamed/conflicted files and nested directories. Add e2e logging for filter changes, tree toggles, stage-all reversibility, ignore side effects, and directory-entry behavior under large trees.

title: clone lazygit main-panel staging, merge, and search semantics
description:
Clone lazygit's main-panel staging, patch-mode, merge-view, and search semantics. This is the interaction model reached after entering the files surface, and it is still thinner than upstream in several ways.

Required clone scope:
- exact `Tab` / `Esc` behavior between files and main panel
- hunk navigation, line/range selection, and stage/pick semantics in staging and merge modes
- line-vs-hunk mode toggling, edit-hunk flows, and conflict navigation where lazygit provides them
- search behavior inside main-panel modes
- truthful distinction between normal, staging, merging, and patch-building submodes so the operator always knows what `Space`, `d`, `a`, `v`, and arrow keys will do
design: This task should finish the main-view feel, not just add isolated shortcuts. Keep the staging/merge/pick semantics Git-correct and make sure the mode transitions are obvious in both rendering and help text.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the main panel behaves close enough to lazygit that a user can move between files, staging, merge resolution, and search flows with the same mental model they already use upstream.
notes: Add end-to-end coverage for entering the main panel from files, staging hunks/lines, switching sides, resolving representative merge conflicts, and returning safely to the files surface with detailed logs.

close lazygit shell layout and mouse-interaction parity gaps
description: Implement the remaining lazygit repo-shell parity work that is still missing above the already-open Unicode graph bead. The current Rust app already exposes many repo subviews in state and it has keyboard-only screen-mode cycling, but the visible shell is still a simplified custom layout: one `Repository shell` header with a flat tab row and a three-pane body (`unstaged`, `staged`, `detail`). Upstream lazygit instead renders five side windows (`status`, `files`, `branches`, `commits`, `stash`) with tab groups inside those windows, mouse bindings, tab click switching, focused side-panel enlargement, and half/full side-window takeover behavior. Local evidence: `crates/tui/src/lib.rs:2863-2874`, `crates/tui/src/lib.rs:2900-2929`, `crates/tui/src/lib.rs:7142-7171`, and `crates/core/src/event.rs:17-21`. Upstream evidence: `references/lazygit-master/pkg/gui/controllers/helpers/window_arrangement_helper.go:421-497`, `references/lazygit-master/pkg/gui/keybindings.go:181-205`, `references/lazygit-master/pkg/gui/keybindings.go:460-468`, and `references/lazygit-master/pkg/gui/view_helpers.go:60-110`. Build the lazygit-style grouped side-panel shell, add mouse plumbing and click routing, match lazygit side-window screen modes, and keep the Files/Worktrees/Submodules cluster stable and smooth when switching tabs or refreshing data.
design: Core/state should model grouped side windows, focused side window, per-group active tab, and mouse event types. TUI/layout should replace the flat repo shell with a five-window lazygit-like arrangement. Input routing should cover click-to-focus, click-to-switch-tab, and maximize/fullscreen behavior. Hardening should preserve list selections/filters and reduce loading churn in the Files/Worktrees/Submodules cluster.
acceptance_criteria: Repo shell renders five lazygit-style side windows instead of a single global tab strip. Grouped tabs for Files/Worktrees/Submodules, Branches/Remotes/Tags, and Commits/Reflog render in the correct windows. Mouse click support exists for focusing panels and clicking tab headers. Half/full screen for side windows behaves like lazygit's enlarged side-window model rather than only a generic detail fullscreen. Files/Worktrees/Submodules switching keeps state stable and avoids repeated placeholder churn during normal tab changes. Existing keyboard flows for already-landed subviews continue to pass.
notes: Testing must cover grouped-panel focus/tab state, mouse events, grouped-panel render output, click-to-switch-tab and click-to-focus behavior, enlarged/full side-window modes, and an e2e/runtime regression around the Files/Worktrees/Submodules cluster because that is the highest-signal UX complaint.

add short slg launcher/build target without breaking super-lazygit compatibility
description:
Add a short developer-facing way to build and run the app so contributors do not need to keep typing the long `super-lazygit` binary name during local iteration. The current app binary is named `super-lazygit`, which is explicit but high-friction for repeated cargo run / target invocation loops.

## Why
The current binary name is long enough that it creates unnecessary typing overhead in normal dev loops. This is a local productivity issue, not a clone-parity issue, but it is worth fixing because it affects every implementation and verification pass.

## Scope
Evaluate the safest short-name strategy and land one canonical approach:
- preferred outcome: support `slg` as the short binary/launcher name for local build and run flows
- preserve backwards compatibility for `super-lazygit` unless there is a strong reason to make a hard rename now
- decide between one of these concrete implementations: secondary Cargo bin name, thin wrapper launcher script, install alias, or explicit cargo alias documented in repo tooling
- update any build/run docs, helper scripts, and developer instructions so one short command becomes the recommended default

## Design constraints
- do not silently break existing scripts, docs, or release expectations that still reference `super-lazygit`
- if both names are kept, make `slg` the recommended short path and `super-lazygit` the compatibility path

title: close remaining lazygit repo-interior clone-1:1 gaps
description:
Close the remaining repo-interior clone-1:1 gaps against upstream lazygit after the first parity pass. The project now has the major panel families and shared navigation contract, but an exact audit still leaves residual advanced-action gaps inside commits, branches, remote refs, status, reflog, tags, and submodules.

This epic now represents the second-pass interior work: taking the panel families that already exist and finishing the advanced actions, option menus, copy/browser/difftool affordances, log-selection flows, and exact semantics that lazygit users rely on once the basic panel is already present.
acceptance_criteria: Done when every remaining repo-interior lazygit clone gap is represented by an open child bead, especially advanced actions that were left out of the first pass; reviewers should be able to trust that finishing this epic and `supergit-1ae` means the repo-mode UI/UX backlog is exhaustive rather than approximate.

title: implement contextual side-panel navigation contract for repo detail views
description:
Implement the shared navigation contract that lazygit repeats across side-panel views and that the current app still only supports in fragments. Right now the repo interior uses numbered subviews plus a few ad hoc shortcuts, while lazygit relies heavily on a contextual pattern where `Enter` drills deeper, `0` returns focus to the main view, `w` jumps to worktree-related flows, and `/` filters the current panel.

Why this matters:
- this is the main reason the app still looks fine but does not feel like lazygit
- if each panel solves `Enter`/`0`/`/` in isolation, behavior will drift again and tests will become panel-specific instead of contractual
- several remaining parity tasks depend on a reusable abstraction for drill-down transitions, focus handoff, and per-panel filtering

Scope:
- define the reducer and TUI routing contract for contextual side-panel drill-down and focus return
- decide how the current 3-pane model maps honestly onto lazygit's "main view" concept without creating confusing fake parity
- add reusable state and helpers for panel-local search/filter where appropriate
- document the contract so future panels (tags, remotes, remote branches, submodules) reuse it rather than inventing bespoke semantics

Testing obligations:
- reducer tests for focus handoff, subview switching, and return behavior
- TUI route tests for repeated contract keys on multiple subviews
- snapshot/render tests proving the operator can discover the contract from on-screen help
- at least one end-to-end script with detailed logging showing a complete drill-down and return cycle

Acceptance criteria:
- there is one shared implementation path for contextual `Enter` / `0` / `w` / `/` semantics where applicable
- panel-specific parity beads can build on this instead of re-implementing the model
- docs explain the resulting semantics without pretending the current architecture is identical to upstream

title: add tags panel and release-reference parity
description:
Add the tags panel that upstream lazygit exposes and that the current app still lacks. Tags are currently invisible in the repository interior, which leaves a significant reference-management hole compared with lazygit.

Required parity scope:
- list tags with useful metadata
- `Space` checkout detached HEAD at tag
- `Enter` view commits reachable from the tag
- `w` worktrees, `0` focus-main, `/` filter, where the shared contract applies
- tag actions for create, delete, push, and reset-on-tag flows as admitted by the product boundary

Why this matters:
- tags are a common release and archaeology surface, and their absence is obvious in parity comparisons
- this panel reuses much of the same reference-inspection machinery as branches and remote branches, so it should strengthen the overall architecture rather than remain a silo

Testing obligations:
- backend tests for lightweight and annotated tag discovery plus tag operations
- reducer/TUI tests for panel routing, filtering, and detached-checkout behavior
- e2e script with detailed logging covering create/delete/push flows in fixture repos

Acceptance criteria:
- tags are browsable and actionable as a first-class panel
- checkout and commit drill-down semantics remain Git-correct and well documented

title: add submodules panel and entry-flow parity
description:
Add the submodules panel family that upstream lazygit exposes and that the current app currently does not model at all. Submodules are niche compared with the core history views, but they are still a meaningful gap in any serious lazygit parity backlog.

Required scope:
- detect and list submodules with useful state
- `Enter` into the selected submodule and preserve a clear path back to the parent repository
- actions for remove, update, add new submodule, edit URL, initialize, and bulk options where they are admitted into the product scope
- `/` filter current submodule view

Why this matters:
- users managing polyrepo setups with submodules expect lazygit-grade visibility here
- entering/exiting submodules is a navigation problem that should integrate cleanly with the existing repo-mode model rather than hack around it

Testing obligations:
- fixture-backed integration tests with real submodule repos
- reducer/TUI tests for enter/exit semantics and submodule action routing
- e2e script with detailed logging showing parent-repo return and submodule lifecycle actions

Acceptance criteria:
- submodules are represented as a first-class managed surface
- entry/exit semantics are explicit and tested
- unsupported edge cases are documented rather than silently mishandled

title: add full lazygit clone parity matrix and regression harness
description:
Build the canonical clone-1:1 lazygit parity matrix and regression harness for the full remaining repo-mode backlog, not just the original repo-interior subset. The matrix must become the single source of truth for what upstream lazygit panel/action exists, what the app currently ships, what bead owns the remaining gap, and what tests prove equivalence.

This artifact should cover repo-global controls, shared navigation semantics, files/main-panel behavior, commit-files/custom-patch behavior, and all repo-interior panels. Its job is to prevent future drift and to make it impossible for the project to claim clone-level parity while still hiding missing UX work in undocumented corners.
acceptance_criteria: Done when there is one canonical matrix plus a repeatable scripted regression path that covers the entire remaining lazygit clone backlog; every open parity bead maps to explicit matrix rows; and reviewers can answer "what is still missing?" by reading the matrix instead of re-auditing the UI by hand.

title: clone residual advanced commit-history actions to lazygit parity
description:
Clone the residual advanced commit-history actions that the first parity pass still leaves unimplemented or only partially represented. The panel exists, but lazygit's commit view remains materially deeper than the current shipped behavior.

Required clone scope:
- bisect/options entry, squash, fixup, set-fixup-message, drop, edit, pick, create-fixup, apply-fixups, and move-commit-up/down flows
- base-commit selection, amend-attribute flows, tag-from-commit, log-options, current-branch selection, and reset-copied-selection semantics
- copy/open/browser/difftool affordances and truthful keybinding parity
- exact consistency between direct commits, sub-commits, and commit lists entered from branches, tags, remotes, and reflog
design: Treat this as a second-pass exactness bead for the commits surface. Preserve Git correctness and safety prompts, but do not silently defer advanced actions that lazygit users expect from the commit panel.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the remaining advanced commit-panel actions are either implemented with exact semantics or split into explicit blocker beads, and commit-history no longer has hidden clone-level omissions.
notes: Cover history-rewriting flows with fixture-backed tests and detailed e2e logging. Failure modes to model explicitly: merge/rebase conflicts during commit surgery, impossible fixup targets, out-of-date copied selections, and unsafe destructive operations.

title: clone residual advanced branch and remote-ref actions to lazygit parity
description:
Clone the residual advanced branch, remote-branch, and remotes-management actions that remain after the first parity pass. The panel families exist, but many of lazygit's branch/ref-management affordances are still absent or only loosely modeled.

Required clone scope:
- local-branch actions: git-flow options, PR/create-PR options, copy PR URL, move commits to new branch, force checkout, merge options, rebase onto selected branch, fast-forward, reset options, sort order, new tag from branch context, copy/difftool affordances
- remote-branch actions: copy branch, merge, rebase, set upstream, reset options, sort order, external difftool, and exact key semantics
- remotes-management actions: fork-remote flow plus any remaining exact-action differences from upstream lazygit
design: Keep local branches, remote branches, and remotes aligned so they feel like one coherent reference-management family rather than three partially compatible panels. Preserve safety prompts for destructive or history-rewriting actions.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the remaining branch/ref-management gaps are explicit and implementable, and the operator no longer hits missing advanced actions after entering panels that otherwise look complete.
notes: Use fixture repos with multiple locals, remotes, upstream shapes, PR-like metadata where possible, and divergent histories. Add e2e logs for checkout/rebase/merge/reset/fork flows and ensure help/docs reflect the final exact keymap.

title: clone residual status, reflog, tag, and submodule control surfaces
description:
Clone the residual status, reflog, tag, and submodule control surfaces that still differ from upstream lazygit even after the first panel pass. These are the kinds of gaps that survive because the panel exists, but the long-tail controls are still missing.

Required clone scope:
- status-panel residuals such as config open/edit, update check, recent-repo entry semantics, and any remaining all-branch-log/status shortcuts not yet exact
- reflog residuals such as copied-selection reset, copy/open/browser affordances, current-branch-log selection, and any remaining exact history-control mismatches
- tag residuals such as copy/difftool/reset-menu exactness and any remaining control-surface mismatches
- submodule residuals such as copy-to-clipboard, bulk-options entry, and other missing control-surface semantics beyond the first shipped action set
design: This is the audit-catch-all for second-pass panel families that exist but are not yet clone-level exact. Use it to eliminate the long tail of UI/UX surprises rather than leaving them to future ad hoc audits.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the long-tail controls for status, reflog, tags, and submodules are either implemented or explicitly split into blocker beads, and the parity matrix has no unowned rows for these panel families.
notes: Add focused reducer/TUI tests for the missing controls plus at least one multi-surface e2e script that traverses status -> reflog/tags/submodules and proves the residual controls, help text, and safety prompts all stay in sync.

title: add status-panel all-branch-logs graph parity
description:
Add the missing status-panel graph/log-history surface that upstream lazygit exposes through `a` and `A` to show or cycle all branch logs. The current app has no equivalent graph view, and this is one of the clearest remaining feature gaps when users compare the product to lazygit.

Why this matters:
- users explicitly notice the absence of graph/log history
- graph exploration is a high-value archaeology workflow that reduces context switching to external `git log --graph` commands
- this is a foundational lazygit identity feature, not a cosmetic shortcut

Scope:
- add a graph/log-history mode reachable from status with `a` and reverse cycling with `A`
- define state for forward/reverse graph ordering and selection behavior
- add backend query support for all-branch graph history that is performant enough for medium repositories
- design how the graph view hands off to commit drill-down or commit-file exploration
- update docs and on-screen help to explain the mode clearly

Testing obligations:
- backend fixture tests for graph output across branching histories
- reducer tests for `a` / `A` cycling and exit behavior
- TUI render tests covering graph visibility, reverse ordering, and selected commit display
- end-to-end script with detailed logging in a repo containing multiple branches and merges

Acceptance criteria:
- the operator can enter graph mode from status, cycle direction, navigate entries, and leave without losing orientation
- graph mode is documented and covered by regression tests
- performance remains acceptable for representative medium-history repos

title: finish local-branches navigation and action parity
description:
Finish local-branches parity beyond the shortcuts already landed. Current work covers `Space` checkout, previous-branch checkout, checkout-by-name, and the worktree jump on `w`, but upstream lazygit still offers a deeper local-branches workflow: `Enter` views commits, `0` returns focus to the main view, `/` filters the current view, and several advanced branch actions are still absent.

Remaining branch-local gaps to close:
- contextual `Enter` drill-down to commit history for the selected branch
- honest `0` and `/` behavior once the shared navigation contract exists
- advanced branch actions still missing from the current product surface: force checkout, rebase onto selected branch, merge options, fast-forward from upstream, reset options, sort order, new tag from branch context, git-flow/PR-related actions where they fit the product boundary

Why this matters:
- branches are one of the first places users feel the semantic mismatch between "action key" and "drill-down key"
- local branch management is central to lazygit's daily workflow, not an optional power-user add-on

Testing obligations:
- route tests for branch drill-down, filtering, focus return, and advanced actions
- reducer/backend tests for each destructive or history-rewriting branch flow
- TUI render tests ensuring branch help text remains truthful as semantics shift
- detailed e2e script covering branch inspection, branch action execution, and recovery paths

Acceptance criteria:
- branch detail semantics are consistent with the shared navigation contract
- advanced branch actions that are admitted into scope are fully wired, documented, and safely tested
- no stale help text remains claiming old `Enter` behavior once drill-down lands

title: finish commit-history drill-down, file explorer, and detached-checkout parity
description:
Finish commit-history parity against upstream lazygit, including the commit-list surfaces reached both directly from the repo interior and indirectly from other reference panels. The current commits view already supports several repo-history actions, but the backlog should treat commit browsing as a shared surface family: direct commits view, branch/tag/remote-branch/reflog "view commits" flows, and the changed-files explorer reached from those commit lists.

Known gaps to address:
- `Space` detached-HEAD checkout of the selected commit
- contextual `Enter` drill-down from commit lists into the selected commit's changed files
- `w` jump to worktree options, `0` focus-main, and `/` current-view filtering where the shared contract applies
- missing commit-panel actions still absent from the product surface: create branch off commit, open commit in browser where applicable, copy commit attributes, select current branch logs, and other admitted lazygit commit-view affordances
- sub-commit parity for commit lists opened from branches, remote branches, tags, reflog, and future graph/history views so they do not invent bespoke behavior
- commit-files explorer parity for the file list reached from commit or stash inspection, including honest scoping for file checkout/open/edit/filter/tree navigation and any explicitly deferred custom-patch behaviors

Why this matters:
- commit archaeology is one of lazygit's signature strengths
- several other parity beads depend on a reusable commit-history browser rather than one-off `Enter` handlers
- without explicit commit-files and sub-commit scope, agents may stop after opening a shallow list that still feels far thinner than lazygit

Testing obligations:
- reducer and Git-backend tests for detached checkout, branch-off-commit flows, and shared commit-list routing
- TUI route tests for drill-down, filtering, worktree jumps, and reused commit-history surfaces reached from multiple parent panels
- render tests for commit help text, sub-commit context, and changed-file explorer help copy
- e2e script with detailed logging covering commit browsing, branch/tag/reflog entry into commit history, file drill-down, and detached checkout recovery

Acceptance criteria:
- commit-list behavior is consistent whether entered from the main commits view or from another reference panel
- changed-files drill-down is discoverable, stable, and honest about any intentionally deferred file-level parity
- advanced history actions are Git-correct, explicit about risk, and regression-tested
acceptance_criteria:
- commit-list behavior is consistent whether entered from the main commits view or from another reference panel
- changed-files drill-down is discoverable, stable, and honest about any intentionally deferred file-level parity
- advanced history actions are Git-correct, explicit about risk, and regression-tested

title: finish stash drill-down and filtering parity
description:
Finish stash-view contextual parity. Recent work landed `Space` apply, `n` new branch, `r` rename, and `w` worktrees, but upstream lazygit still gives the stash panel a richer navigation model: `Enter` views the files in the stash, `0` returns focus to the main view, and `/` filters the current stash list.

Why this matters:
- stash is one of the cleanest places to see the remaining difference between "perform action" and "inspect deeper"
- users often need to inspect stash contents before deciding whether to apply, pop, or branch from them

Scope:
- add stash-file drill-down on `Enter`
- add panel-local filtering and honest focus-return behavior once the shared contract lands
- preserve existing apply/pop/drop/rename/new-branch flows without overloading `Enter`
- ensure stash help copy reflects the new semantics precisely

Testing obligations:
- backend tests for stash-file enumeration and edge cases like renamed/deleted paths
- reducer tests for stash drill-down, filter lifecycle, and return behavior
- TUI route/render tests for the updated stash contract
- e2e script with detailed logging in a fixture repo containing multiple stashes

Acceptance criteria:
- the operator can inspect stash contents before acting on them
- apply/pop/drop/new-branch flows still behave correctly after `Enter` becomes an inspection path
- docs and tests accurately reflect the final stash contract

title: finish reflog history actions and contextual parity
description:
Finish reflog-view parity against upstream lazygit. The current app only exposes reflog selection and restore, but upstream lazygit treats reflog as a real history panel with both commit-style actions and contextual navigation. The backlog needs to reflect that this is not just a drill-down gap.

Known parity scope to evaluate and, where accepted, implement:
- `Space` detached-HEAD checkout of the selected reflog target
- contextual `Enter` flow from reflog entries into the appropriate commit/history view rather than a vague best-effort file jump
- `w` worktrees, `0` focus-main, and `/` current-view filtering under the shared navigation contract
- history actions still absent from the current product surface where they make sense for reflog targets: create branch off selected entry, reset options, copy/cherry-pick style flows, copy commit attributes, open commit in browser, and current-branch-log selection or clearly documented intentional omissions
- preserve the explicit restore flow and its safety messaging without conflicting with the broader history affordances
- define honest behavior for reflog entries that do not map cleanly to a normal commit or branch-backed history view

Why this matters:
- reflog is one of the app's strongest safety and archaeology surfaces, but right now it feels much thinner than lazygit's history tooling
- users comparing the app to lazygit will notice that reflog currently behaves like a single-purpose recovery list instead of a first-class history panel
- accurate scope here avoids agents closing the bead after adding only `Enter` and `/` while major reflog action parity is still missing

Testing obligations:
- fixture-backed backend tests for reflog entries with different target shapes and recovery states
- reducer tests for navigation, filtering, restore coexistence, and action routing on reflog targets
- render tests covering updated help text and edge-case reflog labels
- e2e script with detailed logging for reflog exploration, detached checkout, branch-from-entry, and restore safety checks

Acceptance criteria:
- reflog view supports both recovery and history-inspection workflows without confusing the operator
- admitted reflog actions are Git-correct, discoverable, and regression-tested
- unsupported reflog-entry cases are handled explicitly rather than failing silently
- help/docs remain truthful about what `Space`, `Enter`, `u`, and related history actions now do
acceptance_criteria:
- reflog view supports both recovery and history-inspection workflows without confusing the operator
- admitted reflog actions are Git-correct, discoverable, and regression-tested
- unsupported reflog-entry cases are handled explicitly rather than failing silently
- help/docs remain truthful about what `Space`, `Enter`, `u`, and related history actions now do

title: add worktrees filtering parity
description:
Complete worktrees-view parity by adding the missing current-view filtering/search behavior and aligning the panel with the shared side-panel contract. The core worktree actions are now present, but upstream lazygit still exposes `/` filtering and the broader side-panel navigation pattern.

Why this matters:
- this is a smaller gap than the missing drill-down panels, but it is part of the repeated lazygit interaction language
- worktrees often become noisy in repos with many ephemeral review branches, so filtering materially improves usability

Scope:
- add worktree-list filtering/search on `/`
- ensure selection preservation, clear filter empty states, and predictable exit behavior
- align worktree help/docs with the shared contextual contract if additional keys are admitted later

Testing obligations:
- reducer tests for worktree filter query lifecycle and selection behavior
- TUI route/render tests for filtered lists and empty states
- e2e script with detailed logging using a fixture repo with multiple worktrees

Acceptance criteria:
- the operator can filter the worktree list and recover cleanly
- worktree switching/open/create/remove flows continue to work correctly while filtered

title: add remote-branches panel and action parity
description:
Add the remote-branches panel family that upstream lazygit exposes and that the current app does not yet ship at all. This is a structural parity gap rather than a missing shortcut: there is no current place to inspect remote branches, act on them, or drill from them into commit history.

Required capabilities to evaluate and, where accepted, implement:
- list remote branches with selection state and useful metadata
- `Space` checkout semantics for creating a local branch from a remote branch or checking it out detached when appropriate
- `Enter` view commits, `w` worktrees, `0` focus-main, `/` filter, consistent with the shared contract
- remote-branch actions lazygit exposes: new branch, merge, rebase, delete remote branch, set upstream, reset options, sort order, external diff integration where it fits product boundaries

Why this matters:
- remote refs are a first-class part of lazygit's repo interior and their absence makes the reference-management story incomplete

Testing obligations:
- backend fixture tests for remote-branch discovery and action plumbing
- reducer/TUI tests for navigation, filtering, and selected-action routing
- e2e script with detailed logging using a repo fixture with multiple remotes and remote branches

Acceptance criteria:
- remote branches exist as a first-class panel with keyboard flows that feel native to the rest of the app
- admitted actions are Git-correct, documented, and safely tested

title: add remotes management panel parity
description:
Add the remotes panel that upstream lazygit exposes for viewing and operating on configured remotes. The current app has transport actions from repo mode, but not a dedicated remotes management surface.

Required parity surface:
- list remotes with selection and metadata
- `Enter` view branches for the selected remote
- actions for new remote, remove remote, edit remote, fetch remote, and fork-remote flow where it fits product scope
- `/` filter behavior and consistent integration with the shared panel-navigation contract

Why this matters:
- lazygit treats remotes as browsable and manageable first-class objects, not just hidden config behind fetch/pull/push buttons
- this panel is the natural anchor for remote-specific workflows and for composing with the remote-branches panel

Testing obligations:
- backend tests for remote enumeration and CRUD/update flows using temp repos
- reducer/TUI tests for panel navigation, filter lifecycle, and action routing
- e2e script with detailed logging covering add/edit/remove/fetch flows and failure reporting

Acceptance criteria:
- remotes are visible and manageable from inside the app
- the panel composes cleanly with remote-branches exploration
- destructive operations remain explicit and reversible where Git allows it

title: add git editor commit and reword parity
add branch space checkout parity
add stash pop action parity
Load app config from disk and apply runtime config resolution
add stash rename action parity
add branch worktree shortcut parity
add keep-index stash option and true lazygit unstaged stash semantics
Add bulk submodule options parity with lazygit
Port super_lazygit_rust into a workspace-first Lazygit-grade Rust TUI
description: Create the canonical execution graph for porting the planned product into `super_lazygit_rust`: a workspace-first Rust Git TUI whose repo interior mirrors Lazygit closely while workspace mode borrows the strongest multi-repo ideas from git-dash and git-scope. This root epic exists so every future bead can anchor to one explicit product contract: do not build a generic Git TUI, do not regress into a loose clone, and do not let workspace mode or repo mode drift away from the plan. The graph under this epic must preserve architectural rigor, Git correctness, testing discipline, and performance constraints for a large and precise port.
design: This root epic is the portfolio-level contract for the entire port. It exists to keep three product truths locked together throughout execution: repo mode should feel very close to Lazygit inside a single repository, workspace mode should solve the user's multi-repo pain by combining the strongest structural ideas from git-dash and git-scope, and the whole application should stay within a Rust architecture that can support realtime invalidation, testing, and future extensions without turning into a monolith. Every child epic should preserve these truths instead of optimizing a slice in isolation.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the open graph under this root can carry the implementation from empty scaffold to a credible `super_lazygit_rust` release without needing to reopen plan-space for major missing features, missing tests, or missing architectural seams. The finished graph must preserve Lazygit-grade repo UX, workspace-first multi-repo workflows, realtime freshness behavior, and a disciplined verification story.
notes: Treat this as a precision port, not a vague inspiration project. If a later implementation choice would trade away Lazygit interaction quality, remove multi-repo usefulness, or weaken Git safety without an explicit product reason, that is a graph-level regression and should be corrected before coding proceeds far.

title: Foundation: Rust workspace, app shell, reducer model, and test harnesses
description: Build the foundational Rust workspace and application skeleton for `super_lazygit_rust`. This epic covers the non-negotiable structural substrate: workspace crate layout, bootstrap path, reducer/event model shell, configuration skeleton, diagnostics hooks, and fixture harnesses. Without this, later work on workspace mode and Lazygit-style repo mode will either sprawl into a god-struct or entangle rendering with Git semantics.
design: Separate concerns early: bootstrap and reducer contracts belong in the app/core lane, rendering primitives belong in the tui lane, Git semantics stay behind the git lane, workspace orchestration stays outside rendering, and fixture support must be strong enough that later parity work can prove behavior instead of hand-waving it. This epic is intentionally front-loaded because every later area depends on having crisp boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the workspace crates, reducer contracts, app shell skeleton, theme and keybinding substrate, diagnostics hooks, and fixture helpers exist with enough clarity that later feature work can land without architectural churn. The foundation should already support unit tests, smoke tests, and controlled fixture-driven integration tests.
notes: The failure mode here is hidden coupling: if UI code can freely reach Git backends, if state is ad hoc instead of reducer-driven, or if tests require bespoke shell setup, the later port will slow down and drift. Keep this epic strict even if it feels less visible than feature work.
close_reason: Verified complete against current code and passing workspace test/format gates

title: Scaffold Cargo workspace and planned crate boundaries
description: Create the actual Cargo workspace skeleton for `super_lazygit_rust` with the crate boundaries defined in the plan: app, core, tui, git, workspace, config, and test-support. This task is the first concrete implementation step because every later bead assumes those crates exist and have clear responsibility lines.
design: Create the workspace root manifest and each crate with minimal compileable shells. Keep cross-crate dependencies intentionally narrow from the start so later features do not collapse into a god-crate. Ensure there is no repo-root `main.rs` and that crate names reflect the architectural lanes from the plan.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the workspace builds, every planned crate exists, and a new contributor can identify where bootstrap, state, rendering, Git access, workspace orchestration, config, and fixtures belong.
notes: Include basic compilation smoke tests and CI-friendly commands. Failure mode to avoid: creating placeholder crates with circular dependencies that later lock the architecture into an unhealthy shape.

title: Define core app state, action enums, effects, and reducer contracts
description: Translate the plan's state model into real Rust types. This task defines AppState, WorkspaceState, RepoModeState, action/event enums, effect types, and reducer boundaries so the app can evolve through deterministic state transitions instead of ad hoc widget mutations.
design: Keep business state in . Split domain state from ephemeral view state. The reducer should be able to consume input, worker, watcher, and timer events and emit state plus effects without directly performing I/O.
acceptance_criteria: Done when reducer-driven mode transitions and placeholder state updates compile cleanly and can be unit-tested without the TUI layer.
notes: Add focused unit tests for representative transitions such as enter-repo, leave-repo, selection changes, and overlay open/close. Failure mode to avoid: allowing widgets to own authoritative Git state.

title: Build the initial app shell, mode router, and pane focus model
description: Create the terminal shell that can host workspace mode, repo mode, and modal overlays. This is the UI frame that later features plug into, so it must establish stable focus semantics, mode routing, resize behavior, and a bottom-line status/help region from the start.
design: Use ratatui and crossterm in a way that keeps rendering separate from business state. Define pane IDs, focus transitions, and modal routing explicitly rather than burying them in event handlers.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the app can boot into a stable shell, switch between placeholder modes, preserve focus identity, and render a consistent bottom status/help line.
notes: Add smoke tests or snapshot tests for initial layout states if feasible. Failure mode to avoid: mode routing logic hidden in the rendering layer.

title: Establish config, theme, and keybinding skeletons
description: Create the configuration model for workspace roots, ignores, editor command, theme defaults, and future keybinding overrides. This task exists early so later beads do not hardcode values that should live in config.
design: Implement a minimal config crate with strong defaults, a clear file location, and a schema that can grow without breaking the user. Theme and keybinding support can start skeletal, but action IDs should already be explicit so later customization does not require rewiring the app.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the app can load defaults plus an optional config file and expose typed settings to other crates without global mutable hacks.
notes: Add unit tests for default loading and malformed-config handling. Failure mode to avoid: premature over-configuration that makes the product harder to reason about.

title: Add diagnostics, logging, and performance timing hooks
description: Install the observability hooks needed to understand startup time, scan cost, Git operation latency, watcher churn, and rendering hotspots. This work must happen early because later performance and failure analysis depend on it.
design: Expose structured logging and optional debug timing around scan, refresh, diff load, and long Git operations. Keep the instrumentation cheap in normal mode and richer in debug mode.
acceptance_criteria: Done when there is a consistent way to inspect startup timing, worker latency, and watcher health without editing the code ad hoc during every investigation.
notes: Include at least one diagnostic smoke path and ensure logs do not spam by default. Failure mode to avoid: invisible performance regressions that only surface after many features land.

title: Create temp-repo fixture and topology helpers in test-support
description: Build the `test-support` helpers that can rapidly create clean repos, dirty repos, staged and unstaged mixes, upstream divergence, conflicts, stashes, and worktrees. Nearly every later bead depends on synthetic Git topologies to test correctness without depending on manual shell setup.
design: Provide high-level helpers for repo init, file writes, commits, branch creation, upstream simulation, and conflict generation. Keep these helpers ergonomic enough that future tests use them by default instead of copy-pasting raw Git commands.
acceptance_criteria: Done when downstream beads can create representative Git scenarios through reusable helpers rather than bespoke setup in each test.
notes: Cover at least clean, dirty, staged, and ahead/behind fixtures immediately. Failure mode to avoid: a weak fixture layer that makes precise Git tests too painful to write.

title: Build the effect-executor runtime loop and worker bridge
description: Add the missing runtime layer that consumes reducer effects, executes workspace/git jobs off the render path, and feeds worker/timer/watcher results back into the reducer. This task closes the gap between the current strong state/reducer shell and a usable app runtime, preventing later workspace/repo features from re-implementing ad hoc orchestration.

comments.author: quangdang46
comments.text: Explore-phase graph correction: the current codebase already has a strong reducer/state shell () and a static TUI shell (), but there is no runtime that consumes s, drives background Git/workspace jobs, or feeds worker/timer/watcher events back into the reducer. This bead makes that missing bridge explicit so later workspace refresh, watcher, and real repo interaction work do not each invent their own orchestration path.

title: Git engine parity: facade, backend routing, and safe Git semantics
description: Create the Git engine and parity layer that will let `super_lazygit_rust` behave like real Git while still staying fast enough for multi-repo workspace use. This epic covers the `GitFacade`, backend routing between `gix`, `git2`, and Git CLI, and the explicit parity decisions needed for status, diff, commit, fetch, pull, push, and patch-oriented operations.
design: Use this epic to create a stable Git seam that can support both fast workspace summaries and richer repo-detail interactions without leaking backend-specific types into the UI. Reads, writes, parity quirks, and fallback rules should all become explicit contracts. The engine should serve the Lazygit-like UX rather than forcing the UI to understand low-level Git library details.
acceptance_criteria: Done when callers can obtain summary data, detailed repo views, and write-operation results through stable facade contracts, when backend mismatch behavior is documented and testable, and when the selected Git backend strategy is strong enough to support later staging, sync, history, and rebase flows without redesign.
notes: This epic is one of the main architectural chokepoints in the graph. If the facade is underspecified, later repo mode and history mode beads will compensate with UI-specific hacks. Keep failure surfaces, progress reporting, and backend fallback semantics explicit.

title: Define the GitFacade traits and backend-routing contract
description: Create the facade API that isolates all callers from direct `git2`, `gix`, or Git CLI access. This task sets the permanent seam for status reads, detail reads, diffs, and write operations and therefore determines whether later work remains coherent.
design: Model separate read and write responsibilities, normalize result types, and encode operation progress/error shapes that the UI can consume. Be explicit about which calls are summary-only, detail-only, or write-capable.
acceptance_criteria: Done when other crates can depend on facade traits and typed result models without importing backend-specific types.
notes: Add interface-level tests or compile checks that enforce the no-backend-leak rule. Failure mode to avoid: UI code reaching around the facade because the contract is incomplete.

title: Implement workspace summary readers for branch, dirty, ahead/behind, and fetch age
description: Implement the high-volume read path used by workspace mode. This task should produce lightweight RepoSummary data including branch, dirty counts, ahead/behind, remote shorthand, and fetch-age information so the dashboard can become useful quickly.
design: Prefer fast local reads and summary-oriented backends. Normalize no-upstream, detached HEAD, inaccessible repo, and never-fetched cases so the UI does not need special ad hoc logic.
acceptance_criteria: Done when workspace mode can obtain trustworthy repo summaries quickly across many repositories and represent edge cases explicitly instead of flattening them into false-clean states.
notes: Back this with fixture-driven tests for clean, dirty, detached, ahead, behind, diverged, and missing-upstream repos. Failure mode to avoid: silently misreporting sync state.

title: Implement repo detail readers for status trees, diffs, branches, commits, stash, and reflog
description: Implement the heavier read APIs needed by repo mode and history/reference views. This includes working tree status, staged status, diff inputs, branch lists, commit lists, stash lists, reflog items, and worktree metadata where practical.
design: Use one normalized set of domain models so status, diff, history, and branch views can compose instead of each building their own bespoke parser stack. Be explicit about pagination or lazy-load boundaries where large repos may matter.
acceptance_criteria: Done when repo mode can obtain structured detail data without shelling out from the UI layer and without each view inventing its own data source contract.
notes: Cover representative fixtures including staged/unstaged mixes, stash presence, branch lists, and reflog data. Failure mode to avoid: detail models that are view-specific and impossible to reuse.

title: Implement core write operations for commit, checkout, fetch, pull, and push
description: Implement the first set of write operations that the product needs for daily usefulness: commit, amend head if straightforward, branch checkout, fetch, pull, and push. These operations must surface progress and errors cleanly and must preserve Git semantics that users already trust.
design: Route through the facade, not directly from views. Prefer Git CLI where hooks, credential helpers, or transport semantics matter. Ensure write results trigger the right follow-up refresh behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Done when core write flows can execute from facade tests with explicit success/failure results and enough metadata for UI confirmations and toasts.
notes: Add integration tests for missing upstream, auth-free local remotes where possible, branch checkout, and commit flows. Failure mode to avoid: fire-and-forget writes that leave the UI in stale state.

title: Run a patch and hunk-application parity spike for selective staging
description: Investigate and implement the backend path for hunk-level staging and unstaging. This is a parity spike because selective staging is central to the port, but backend semantics can drift badly between libraries and Git CLI.
design: Prototype the chosen path behind the facade, document edge cases like reverse patching and untracked files, and determine whether , CLI patch application, or a hybrid route is safest.
acceptance_criteria: Done when there is a clearly chosen selective-staging path with fixture-backed evidence and explicit notes about unsupported or deferred edge cases.
notes: Include tests for multi-hunk files, reverse apply behavior, and failure cases. Failure mode to avoid: claiming hunk parity without reliable patch semantics.

title: Codify backend mismatch rules, fallback paths, and parity tests
description: Capture the places where , , and Git CLI do not behave identically and turn them into executable safeguards. This task exists so the port remains precise over time rather than relying on remembered tribal knowledge.
design: Write explicit tests and decision notes for mismatch cases. Where behavior is uncertain, force the facade to choose a conservative fallback instead of letting callers guess.
acceptance_criteria: Done when risky mismatch areas have named tests or documented fallback rules and future contributors can see why one backend was chosen over another.
notes: Failure mode to avoid: silently regressing from Git-correct behavior after a later refactor.

title: Workspace mode: discovery, dashboard, search, filters, and triage UX
description: Implement workspace mode as the product's command center. This epic covers discovery, cached startup, repo registry, worker-based refresh, table rendering, search/filter/sort, preview panes, and workspace switching. The goal is to solve the user's multi-repo blindness before repo-mode depth work even begins.
design: Workspace mode should not be a decorative repo picker. It should give the user a durable command center for many repositories: discovery, caching, refresh orchestration, searchable and sortable repo tables, preview context, and graceful handling of incomplete scans. The information density and keyboard flow should feel intentional and consistent with the Lazygit visual language even though the layout is more dashboard-like.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the app can discover repositories from configured roots, preserve stable identities, render a useful workspace table, support fast triage through search/filter/sort, preview the selected repo, and survive loading or partial-failure states without confusing the operator. Transitioning from workspace mode into repo mode should feel immediate and predictable.
notes: The core product differentiation lives here. Avoid building a generic list plus metadata dump. The user needs a mode that meaningfully reduces multi-repo context switching, surfaces attention-worthy repositories quickly, and still feels visually and behaviorally consistent with the repo interior.

title: Implement recursive repo discovery with `.git` dir and gitdir-file support
description: Build workspace discovery so the app can scan configured roots, identify Git repositories, respect gitdir files for worktrees/submodules, and stop descending once a repo root has been found. This is the entry point for workspace usefulness.
design: Support canonical path resolution, display-path retention, ignore globs, and nested-repo stop rules. Treat inaccessible directories and broken gitdir files as partial failures instead of whole-scan aborts.
acceptance_criteria: Done when configured roots produce a stable set of repo identities suitable for caching and summary refresh across normal and worktree-style repositories.
notes: Add integration tests for nested repos, gitdir-file repos, and symlink-friendly paths where practical. Failure mode to avoid: scanning into nested repos repeatedly or missing worktree-style repos.

title: Build the workspace cache and stable repo registry
description: Implement the cache-backed startup path and registry that remember previously seen repos, their last known summaries, and the workspace-root fingerprint. This task is what makes workspace mode feel immediate instead of blocked on a cold scan every launch.
design: Store enough metadata to render stale-but-useful summaries quickly, then refresh in background. Keep cache invalidation simple and explicit. The repo registry should provide stable IDs independent of row ordering.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the app can warm-start from cache, detect workspace-root changes, and expose stable repo IDs and summaries to the rest of the system.
notes: Add tests for cache hit, cache miss, stale cache, and root mismatch cases. Failure mode to avoid: cache corruption or path instability causing duplicate repo identities.

title: Implement background summary refresh workers and scheduling
description: Create the worker-pool orchestration that refreshes repo summaries off the UI thread. This task should mirror the pragmatic strength of git-dash while fitting the reducer/effect architecture from the foundation epic.
design: Use bounded worker concurrency, explicit job types, and event-driven completion notifications back into core state. Do not let long-running repos freeze the list or block unrelated refreshes.
acceptance_criteria: Done when summary refresh can run across many repos concurrently with progress/error signals returned to state management cleanly.
notes: Add tests or harness checks for partial failures and worker completion ordering. Failure mode to avoid: hidden thread coordination logic that the reducer cannot reason about.

title: Render the workspace repo table and selection model
description: Implement the dense repo table that becomes the default home screen. This task covers row rendering, selection movement, width-sensitive column presentation, focus behavior, and the basic table-driven visual language for the multi-repo command center.
design: Prioritize repo name, branch, dirty summary, ahead/behind, and fetch age. Keep the table scan-friendly and avoid decorative noise. Preserve enough styling continuity with Lazygit that repo-mode transition later feels natural.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the user can move through a live repo table, understand row state at a glance, and preserve selection across refreshes.
notes: Add snapshot coverage for wide and narrow layouts if practical. Failure mode to avoid: a pretty table that hides the most important operational signals.

title: Add workspace search, filter, sort, and attention-score behavior
description: Implement the triage behaviors that make workspace mode a real command center: fuzzy-ish search, dirty/ahead/behind/conflict filters, sort modes, and a default attention score that surfaces repos needing action first.
design: Keep the rules explicit enough that users can understand why a repo floated upward. Search and filters must not fight each other or destroy orientation.
acceptance_criteria: Done when a user can quickly narrow many repos down to the ones that matter and the default ordering feels operationally smart rather than arbitrary.
notes: Add reducer tests for search/filter/sort combinations and edge cases like no-upstream or error rows. Failure mode to avoid: opaque ranking that undermines trust.

title: Implement selected-repo preview pane and workspace-to-repo transition
description: Build the preview experience for the selected repo and the enter/return flow between workspace mode and repo mode. This task is about orientation: users should see summary context before diving in and should return to the same workspace context after deep work.
design: Support at least one preview mode immediately, with room for recent commits or file-change previews later. Preserve selected row, scroll position, filters, and search when returning from repo mode.
acceptance_criteria: Done when entering repo mode feels like zooming in and returning feels lossless in terms of workspace context.
notes: Include reducer tests for enter/leave transitions and selection restoration. Failure mode to avoid: context loss when hopping between repos.

title: Handle workspace loading, empty, partial-failure, and switching flows
description: Finish the operational states around workspace mode: loading indicators, no-repo states, partial failures, and workspace-root switching. The app must remain comprehensible when scan results are incomplete or empty.
design: Expose scan progress, current root, and clear next actions in empty states. For partial failures, keep successful rows visible and let users inspect broken ones. Workspace switching should be explicit and recoverable.
acceptance_criteria: Done when workspace mode communicates what is happening during scans and does not collapse into ambiguity when repos are missing or broken.
notes: Add snapshot or interaction tests for empty, loading, and partial-failure states. Failure mode to avoid: a blank screen or silent degradation during scan problems.

title: Realtime freshness: watchers, invalidation, debounce, and fallback polling
description: Add realtime freshness to workspace and repo state through watcher-driven invalidation, debounced refresh scheduling, visibility-aware priorities, and fallback polling. This epic exists to solve stale-state trust problems without introducing watcher churn, UI freezes, or high-resource background behavior.
design: Realtime freshness should combine push-style invalidation where possible with pragmatic scheduling and fallback polling where watches degrade. The architecture must recognize that not every filesystem or repository topology is equally watchable, so backend health, debounce policy, visibility-aware refresh, and degraded-mode UX all need first-class treatment instead of being bolted on afterward.
acceptance_criteria: Done when repository state can refresh promptly after real changes, when noisy change storms are debounced into sane refresh work, when active and visible repos receive stronger freshness guarantees than hidden ones, and when degraded watcher environments fall back to polling with clear operator feedback instead of silent staleness.
notes: This epic exists to solve the user's realtime pain without destabilizing the app. The main failure modes are stale state, thrashing refresh loops, and watch behavior that looks magical in demos but breaks in large workspaces. Favor explicit health reporting and measurable freshness semantics.

title: Implement watcher abstraction and backend health reporting
description: Create the watcher abstraction that hides backend details and emits normalized invalidation signals plus health state. This task is the gateway to realtime freshness and must avoid leaking platform-specific watcher quirks into core logic or UI components.
design: Watchers should produce repo-scoped invalidation events and explicit degraded or recovered health signals. Keep the abstraction testable with fake event sources so later debounce and fallback logic can be validated without real filesystem churn.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the system can register watches, emit normalized invalidation events, and report backend health without UI code touching watcher internals.
notes: Add tests around synthetic watcher events and health transitions. Failure mode to avoid: platform-specific watcher assumptions hardcoded into state logic.

title: Build per-repo invalidation debounce and refresh scheduling
description: Implement the debounce layer that converts noisy file events into bounded repo refresh requests. This task is where watcher realism matters: editors and Git internals can generate bursts, so the scheduler must coalesce rather than thrash.
design: Group events by repo ID, debounce aggressively enough to avoid storms, and route refresh jobs back into the same effect pipeline as manual or periodic refreshes. Keep the logic deterministic enough for reducer and scheduler tests.
acceptance_criteria: Done when repeated file events for one repo become a sensible refresh cadence instead of a refresh flood.
notes: Add tests for bursty event sequences and coalescing behavior. Failure mode to avoid: every save event causing multiple redundant refreshes.

title: Add visibility-aware refresh priority for active, visible, and hidden repos
description: Teach the refresh system to prioritize the repo the user is actively viewing, then visible workspace rows, then hidden repos. This task ensures realtime freshness improves trust without wasting resources on repos the user cannot currently see.
design: Use the current mode, selected repo, and visible table window as explicit priority inputs. Integrate with worker scheduling rather than creating a side scheduler that fights the main refresh path.
acceptance_criteria: Done when active repos refresh first, visible repos refresh promptly, and hidden repos can lag without degrading the UX.
notes: Add state and scheduler tests that prove priority ordering. Failure mode to avoid: hidden repos consuming worker capacity while the focused repo waits.

title: Implement fallback polling and freshness badges for degraded watch mode
description: Provide a safe fallback when the watcher backend fails or is unavailable. Users must still trust the dashboard, so the app needs timer-based refresh plus explicit freshness or degraded badges instead of silently going stale.
design: Expose watch health and freshness state in both workspace mode and repo mode. Poll conservatively to preserve low-resource behavior. Make the degraded state obvious but not noisy.
acceptance_criteria: Done when a watcher failure no longer leaves the app silently stale and the user can see whether state is live or on fallback polling.
notes: Add tests for degraded-to-recovered transitions. Failure mode to avoid: stale state presented as live state.

title: Repo mode core parity: status, diff, staging, commit, and sync loop
description: Implement the core repo-mode experience so it feels recognizably close to Lazygit for the main daily loop: inspect working tree, inspect staged set, inspect diff, stage/unstage precisely, commit, and sync. This epic is where the port must become unmistakably useful to Lazygit users.
design: Repo mode should inherit the strongest Lazygit interaction patterns as directly as the new architecture allows. The pane layout, focus traversal, diff reading flow, staging actions, commit box behavior, and sync confirmations should all feel familiar to experienced Lazygit users while remaining internally reducer-driven and testable. This is the core UX parity epic.
acceptance_criteria: Done when a user can enter repo mode and perform the common inner loop of triaging file changes, inspecting diffs, staging and unstaging work, composing commits, and running fetch/pull/push actions through a layout and focus system that feels close to Lazygit rather than merely inspired by it.
notes: Do not let visual similarity substitute for behavioral parity. If pane focus, staging semantics, diff navigation, or sync confirmations feel inconsistent with Lazygit expectations, the port is still incomplete even if screenshots look close.

title: Build repo mode shell and Lazygit-like focus model
description: Create the structural shell for repo mode so the user can move between working tree, staged tree, diff, and overlays in a way that feels close to Lazygit. This task establishes the mental model for all deeper repo interactions.
design: Mirror Lazygit panel relationships closely while fitting the new reducer-based architecture. Keep focus transitions explicit and ensure the bottom line reflects current pane actions and disabled reasons.
